1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	## Memory
26	
27	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
28	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
29	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
30	and it loses on discoverability:
31	
32	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
33	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
34	  (human or bot).
35	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
36	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
37	
38	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
39	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
40	
41	## Speculation marker
42	
43	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
44	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
45	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
46	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
47	tell the measured from the inferred.
48	
49	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
50	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
51	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
52	in the written record.
53	
54	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
55	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
56	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
57	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
58	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
59	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
60	
61	## Committing
62	
63	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
64	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
65	visible at the start of the command.
66	
67	### App repo
68	```
69	jj commit -m \
70	"title" \
71	-m "body
72	
73	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
74	-R .
75	```
76	
77	### Bot session repo
78	```
79	jj commit -m \
80	"title" \
81	-m "body
82	
83	ochid: /<changeID>" \
84	-R .claude
85	```
86	
87	## jj Basics
88	
89	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
90	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
91	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
92	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
93	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
94	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
95	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
96	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
97	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
98	  session because session data updates continuously.
99	
100	## Commit Message Style
101	
102	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
103	a version suffix:
104	
105	```
106	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
107	```
108	
109	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
110	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
111	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
112	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
113	  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
114	  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
115	  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
116	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
117	  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
118	  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
119	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
120	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
121	  in-session work, not code changes.
122	- Examples:
123	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
124	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
125	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
126	
127	## Pre-commit Requirements
128	
129	### User approval
130	
131	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
132	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
133	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
134	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
135	
136	### Review before proposing the commit block
137	
138	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
139	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
140	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
141	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
142	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
143	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
144	
145	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
146	review pause before its commit block appears.
147	
148	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
149	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
150	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
151	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
152	step before starting the next; don't ask.
153	
154	### Notes references
155	
156	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
157	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
158	
159	### Versioning
160	
161	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
162	on scope:
163	
164	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
165	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
166	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
167	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
168	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
169	
170	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
171	starting.
172	
173	For multi-step:
174	
175	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
176	   marker.
177	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
178	   needed).
179	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
180	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
181	   marker.
182	
183	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
184	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
185	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
186	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
187	below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
188	adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
189	doubles typing per commit.
190	
191	The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
192	amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
193	to see which commits were exploratory vs final.
194	
195	### Chores section headers
196	
197	Chores section headers use trailing version format:
198	
199	```
200	## Description (X.Y.Z)
201	```
202	
203	Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``
204	
205	### Pre-commit checklist
206	
207	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
208	
209	1. `cargo fmt`

... [237 lines truncated] ...

content.content:
200	## Description (X.Y.Z)
201	```
202	
203	Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``
204	
205	### Pre-commit checklist
206	
207	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
208	
209	1. `cargo fmt`
210	2. `cargo clippy`
211	3. `cargo test`
212	4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
213	5. Retest after install
214	6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
215	7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
216	8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
217	   new subcommands, changed behavior)
218	
219	## Code Conventions
220	
221	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
222	
223	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
224	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
225	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
226	comment exists, not that it be long.
227	
228	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
229	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
230	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
231	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
232	non-obvious WHY.
233	
234	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
235	
236	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
237	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
238	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
239	
240	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
241	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
242	  self-evident.
243	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
244	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
245	
246	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
247	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
248	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
249	
250	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
251	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
252	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
253	
254	```rust
255	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
256	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
257	
258	match matches.len() {
259	    1 => {
260	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
261	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
262	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
263	    }
264	    // ...
265	}
266	```
267	
268	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
269	correct test behavior.
270	
271	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
272	
273	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
274	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
275	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
276	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
277	
278	### Recognize when stuck
279	
280	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
281	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
282	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
283	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
284	on X."
285	
286	## ochid Trailers
287	
288	Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
289	counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
290	path followed by the changeID:
291	
292	- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
293	- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`
294	
295	Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
296	is app repo, second is `.claude`).
297	
298	## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow
299	
300	**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
301	command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
302	preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
303	composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
304	prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
305	bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
306	local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
307	after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
308	recovery is forward-only.
309	
310	**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
311	Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
312	single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
313	is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
314	commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
315	finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
316	they happen rather than being batched until the end.
317	
318	### Run `vc-x1 push`
319	
320	```
321	vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
322	vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
323	vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
324	vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
325	vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
326	vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
327	vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
328	```
329	
330	The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:
331	
332	1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
333	   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
334	2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
335	   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
336	   reads right".
337	
338	Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
339	only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
340	pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.
341	
342	For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
343	and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.
344	
345	### Bot communication during the flow
346	
347	When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
348	each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
349	etc.):
350	
351	1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
352	   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
353	   something like:
354	
355	   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
356	   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.
357	
358	2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
359	   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
360	   Do **not** spell out the full
361	   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
362	   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
363	   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
364	   debugging).
365	
366	3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
367	   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
368	   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
369	   detached and absolute-last; see "After finalize: stop and
370	   wait" below).
371	
372	### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync` (still useful)
373	
374	Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --no-dry-run` as its first step
375	so divergence is resolved before the build. Running sync manually
376	before you *start* editing is still cheap (one line when clean)
377	and surfaces remote changes earlier:
378	
379	```
380	vc-x1 sync                            # dual-repo workspace
381	vc-x1 sync -R .                       # single-repo project
382	vc-x1 sync --quiet                    # silent; exit code signals result
383	```
384	
385	Output shape:
386	
387	- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all up-to-date`. Proceed.
388	- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`): per-repo fetch +
389	  state lines, then `dry-run — re-run with --no-dry-run to apply`.
390	  Inspect, then re-run with `--no-dry-run` (or resolve conflicts
391	  if rebase fails).
392	- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.
393	
394	### After finalize: stop and wait
395	
396	After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session per-step
397	or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit
398	files, run tools, or emit any text (prose, recaps, acknowledgements),
399	until the user explicitly directs you to continue. Treat finalize as
400	a hard stop for the whole turn. Any final words (e.g. "next is ...")
401	must be said in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the
402	finalize `Bash` call is the last thing in the turn and nothing
403	follows it.
404	
405	This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
406	N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
407	Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a checkpoint
408	they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or take a break at.
409	Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and produces unwanted writes
410	between finalize and the next explicit instruction.
411	
412	Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized at
413	this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.
414	
415	### Late changes after push
416	
417	If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
418	updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
419	`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes in, then re-push:
420	
421	```
422	jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
423	jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .
424	jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
425	```
426	
427	(`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
428	push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
429	writes so you rarely hit this case there.)
430	
431	### Manual finalize fallback
432	
433	If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
434	was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
435	run finalize by hand:
436	
437	```
438	vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
439	```
440	
441	**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no
442	tool calls, no additional output. If any work is done after

file.content:
## Description (X.Y.Z)
```

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

## ochid Trailers

Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
path followed by the changeID:

- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`

Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
they happen rather than being batched until the end.

### Run `vc-x1 push`

```
vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
```

The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:

1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
   reads right".

Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.

For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.

### Bot communication during the flow

When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
etc.):

1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
   something like:

   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.

2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
   Do **not** spell out the full
   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
   debugging).

3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
   detached and absolute-last; see "After finalize: stop and
   wait" below).

### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync` (still useful)

Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --no-dry-run` as its first step
so divergence is resolved before the build. Running sync manually
before you *start* editing is still cheap (one line when clean)
and surfaces remote changes earlier:

```
vc-x1 sync                            # dual-repo workspace
vc-x1 sync -R .                       # single-repo project
vc-x1 sync --quiet                    # silent; exit code signals result
```

Output shape:

- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all up-to-date`. Proceed.
- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`): per-repo fetch +
  state lines, then `dry-run — re-run with --no-dry-run to apply`.
  Inspect, then re-run with `--no-dry-run` (or resolve conflicts
  if rebase fails).
- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session per-step
or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit
files, run tools, or emit any text (prose, recaps, acknowledgements),
until the user explicitly directs you to continue. Treat finalize as
a hard stop for the whole turn. Any final words (e.g. "next is ...")
must be said in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the
finalize `Bash` call is the last thing in the turn and nothing
follows it.

This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a checkpoint
they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or take a break at.
Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and produces unwanted writes
between finalize and the next explicit instruction.

Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized at
this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.

### Late changes after push

If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes in, then re-push:

```
jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

(`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
writes so you rarely hit this case there.)

### Manual finalize fallback

If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no
tool calls, no additional output. If any work is done after
finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.
Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
already displays it.

lastPrompt: Just now I updated CLAUDE.md with the latest version from ../vc-template-x1/. The biggest change is vc-x1 now has a push subcommand so it's not necessary to use jj when commiting/finalizing. Look at i…

content.text:
Read the new CLAUDE.md. A few clarifying questions before I proceed:

1. **`--yes` and the two internal gates.** Push has two approval gates (review + message) surfaced by push itself, but the bot-communication section says to run push with `--yes --title --body` after in-conversation approval. Am I right that `--yes` skips push's internal gates because we've already done equivalent review+approval in the conversation?

2. **Preflight overlap.** Push's preflight runs `sync + fmt + clippy + test` as its first step. Should I skip my own pre-commit checklist now (since push will run it and fail out if anything's broken), or still run `cargo fmt/clippy/test` ahead of time to catch issues before I present work for review? The former saves time; the latter avoids "work complete" followed by a preflight failure.

3. **Body content.** Old flow: app-repo body and session-repo body differed (app = code-change index mirroring chores; session = session-activity notes). New flow says "both titles and bodies are the same; only the `ochid:` trailer differs." So the single shared body is the app-repo-style one-line-per-file index, and session-activity bullets go away. Confirm?

4. **`ochid:` trailer.** Push collects chids internally — so I should NOT put `ochid: /...` at the end of my `--body` argument, push appends the correct trailer per repo itself. Confirm?

5. **`cargo install --path .` (step 4 of the old checklist).** Not called out in push's preflight (which is sync/fmt/clippy/test). Keep running it myself to verify binaries build and report the right `--version`, or drop it?

message.content:
1. that is correct, the non-yes mode is for it can be used manually
2. Continue to run before as iterations are generally needed, the push instance ensures it's been done.
3. Hmm, if there are "off-topic" items in the session it's be good have a separated bulleted section titled `## <$other-repo> session notes:`
4. yes, check source code
5. not sure, check sourc code

After checking the source code update CLAUDE.md here and in ../vc-temmplate-x1 after we agree

content.text:
Source-code findings (all three answers grounded in `vc-x1/src/push.rs`):

- **Preflight (push.rs:784–803)**: runs `vc-x1 sync --no-dry-run`, `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo test`. Nothing else.
- **No `cargo install` / retest in preflight** (push.rs:780–781, explicit comment: *"pre-commit checklist (minus `cargo install` / retest, which are project-specific)"*). So step 4 stays on the bot.
- **Ochid trailer auto-appended** at the commit stages: `commit-app` appends `\n\nochid: /.claude/<claude_chid>` (push.rs:997); `commit-claude` appends `\n\nochid: /<app_chid>` (push.rs:1060). Template comment at push.rs:940: *"Body goes after a blank line. ochid trailers are appended"*. So the body passed via `--body` must **not** include an ochid trailer.
- **`--yes`** auto-approves review (push.rs:828–830) and message gates.

Both `actor-x1/CLAUDE.md` and `vc-template-x1/CLAUDE.md` are byte-identical — one diff applies to both.

**Proposed CLAUDE.md edits** (three, for agreement before applying):

**Edit A — `## Commit Message Style`** (around lines 112–125). Currently two bullets (`App-repo body` / `Session-repo body`) describe different bodies. Replace with three bullets describing the unified body + optional session-notes tail + ochid rule:

- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet = one file, one-line gist, mirroring `notes/chores-*.md`. Chores is the source of truth; the body is a scan-able index.
- **Session notes** (optional tail appended to the body): if the session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends, rationale — append a trailing subsection titled `## .claude session notes:` with those items as bullets. Same body (main content + any session-notes) lands in both commits.
- **Ochid trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you pass via `--body`. Push appends the correct per-repo trailer at commit-app (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>`) and commit-claude (`ochid: /<chid>`).

**Edit B — `### Pre-commit checklist`** (around lines 205–217). Keep the 8-step list as-is, but append a short clarifying paragraph:

> Push's preflight re-runs `vc-x1 sync` + `cargo fmt` + `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` + `cargo test` as a safety net. Run the checklist above locally anyway — fmt/clippy/test iteration is cheaper in-conversation than bouncing off preflight after review approval. Steps 4–5 (`cargo install --path .` and retest) are **not** in push's preflight (project-specific); the bot runs them.

**Edit C — `### Bot communication during the flow`** (around lines 338–340). Currently says:

> Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits; only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.

Minor trim to cross-reference Commit Message Style rather than duplicate the body rules:

> Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits; per-repo `ochid:` trailers are appended by push itself. See `## Commit Message Style` for body structure.

Agree on A / B / C? Any wording you'd tighten before I apply to both files?

content.content:
100	## Commit Message Style
101	
102	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
103	a version suffix:
104	
105	```
106	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
107	```
108	
109	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
110	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
111	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
112	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
113	  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
114	  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
115	  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
116	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
117	  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
118	  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
119	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
120	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
121	  in-session work, not code changes.
122	- Examples:
123	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
124	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
125	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
126	
127	## Pre-commit Requirements
128	
129	### User approval
130	
131	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
132	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
133	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
134	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
135	
136	### Review before proposing the commit block
137	
138	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
139	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
140	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
141	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
142	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
143	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
144	
145	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
146	review pause before its commit block appears.
147	
148	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
149	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
150	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
151	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
152	step before starting the next; don't ask.
153	
154	### Notes references
155	
156	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
157	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
158	
159	### Versioning
160	
161	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
162	on scope:
163	
164	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
165	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.

file.content:
## Commit Message Style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
  in-session work, not code changes.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

### Review before proposing the commit block

After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.

This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
review pause before its commit block appears.

Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
step before starting the next; don't ask.

### Notes references

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
   marker.

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

### Chores section headers

Chores section headers use trailing version format:

```
## Description (X.Y.Z)
```

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the

- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3
  sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds
  one-to-one with the edits structure already documented in
  `notes/chores-*.md` for this step — just the file and a
  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not*
  restate the detail that lives in chores; the commit body is a
  scan-able index, not a duplicate. The chores section is the

- **Session notes** (optional tail, appended to the body): if the
  session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the
  code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends,
  rationale — append a trailing subsection titled
  `## .claude session notes:` with those items as bullets. The
  same body (main content plus any session-notes subsection)
  lands in both commits.
- **Ochid trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you
  pass via `--body`. Push appends the correct per-repo trailer
  at the commit stages (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>` for the app-repo
  commit; `ochid: /<chid>` for the `.claude` commit).

bot runs them.

content.text:
Finding from the source: `push.rs:595-596` has `fn claude_path(root) -> root.join(".claude")` — **push hardcodes `.claude`**, while `fix_desc.rs`, `init.rs`, and friends actually read `[workspace].other-repo` from `.vc-config.toml` (and the template even emits `other-repo = ".."` in some modes, so the config is meant to vary). So CLAUDE.md is describing current reality for push, but the abstraction *is* real for the rest of the tool.

Three ways to clarify, ranked:

1. **Minimal (recommended)** — add a short "Terminology" block near the top defining `other-repo` as `[workspace].other-repo` from `.vc-config.toml` (default `.claude`, used as the concrete example throughout). Leave most existing `.claude` references alone, since they match what push actually does; only reword the few places where the label is conceptual (e.g. the session-notes subsection title could become `## <other-repo> session notes:` in description text, rendered literally as `## .claude session notes:` when composing for a `.claude`-default project).

2. **Full abstraction** — sweep `.claude` → `<other-repo>` throughout, add one "default is `.claude`" note. More correct-looking but misleading given push's hardcoding — readers may assume changing the config will Just Work.

3. **Fix push first, then (2)** — file a vc-x1 issue to teach push to read `other-repo` from config, then document the abstraction honestly. Bigger scope, probably right long-term.

The bot thinks (1) is the right call now: the abstraction exists for most of vc-x1 and is worth surfacing, but push's hardcoding means full abstraction in docs would overstate current behavior. Which flavor do you want — (1), (2), or (3)?

message.content: agreed 1

content.text:
Push halted at `push-app`. Current state:

**Done locally:**
- App commit created: `oxtpuuon 33d15293 chore: plan workspace for this repo (0.2.0-0)`
- `.claude` commit created: `xvwtkuyo c1639253` (same title)
- Both `main` bookmarks advanced to the new commits

**Blocked at `push-app`:**
```
error: Error: Non-tracking remote bookmark main@origin exists
Hint: Run `jj bookmark track main --remote=origin` to import the remote bookmark.
```

**Two issues to resolve, want your call on both:**

1. **Remote tracking.** The hint suggests `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .`. Safe and reversible — just teaches jj to track the existing remote bookmark. After that, `vc-x1 push main --yes --from push-app` resumes the flow.

2. **`.vc-x1/push-state.toml` leaked into the app commit.** Push's own warning at the top said so: *"state dir '.vc-x1' is not in ... `.gitignore` — add a '/.vc-x1' line so in-progress push-state files don't get committed."* The review diff confirms: `.vc-x1/push-state.toml | 6 +` is part of commit `33d15293`. Options:

   - **(a) Fix forward**: add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore` as its own subsequent commit, push `33d15293` as-is. Simplest. Cost: one stale push-state file in history.
   - **(b) Rewrite the commit**: while it's still unpushed, `jj edit @-`, remove the file from the commit (via `jj file untrack` + delete from working copy), add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore` in the same commit, pop back with `jj new`. Clean history. `.claude` commit is untouched. More moving parts but nothing destructive if I'm careful.
   - **(c) Abandon and redo**: abandon both commits, fix `.gitignore` first, start push over. Cleanest reset, but we lose the `.claude` commit which was fine.

The bot thinks **(b)** is right — the commit isn't pushed yet so we can still rewrite it safely, and (a) would permanently bake a spurious state file into history. But (b) needs your approval before I touch the mutable commit, and if (b) goes wrong we fall back to (a).

Your call: (a) / (b) / (c), and go-ahead on the `jj bookmark track` step?

message.content: We need to add .vc-x1 to .gitignore and also make add that info to CLAUDE.md, if it was there you might have caught it. Assuming you agree do both and squash them into the change and let's see how resuming works, push-state.toml has the current state so you should just be able to do `vc-x1 push` and it should "just-work". (Also, don't change vc-template-x1, that'll be on me). Your thoughts, don't do anything until we both agree.

content.text:
Agreed on the approach. Analysis:

**Key insight:** the user's plan doesn't require removing `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` from the existing commit — it only requires `.gitignore` to exclude future changes. The file stays tracked in commit `33d15293` as a one-time scar, but once gitignored, subsequent modifications to it (as push advances through stages) won't leak into future commits. That keeps `push-state.toml` alive on disk for the resume, and keeps the amendment minimal.

**Proposed actions (in order):**

1. **Add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore`** (actor-x1 only, not vc-template-x1).

2. **Add a gitignore note to CLAUDE.md.** Best spot: a short "Prerequisites" paragraph near the top of `## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow`, before `### Run vc-x1 push`:

   > **Prerequisite.** `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's
   > in-progress state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get
   > committed. Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
   > This only needs to be set up once per repo.

3. **Squash both changes into `@-`** (commit `33d15293`) via `jj squash -R .`. Since `/.vc-x1` is in `.gitignore` at snapshot time, the `push-state.toml` modifications push made during the failed run won't appear as tracked changes in `@`, so the squash carries only the two intended edits (`.gitignore` + `CLAUDE.md`). The already-tracked `push-state.toml` inside `33d15293` remains — scar accepted.

4. **Track the remote bookmark**: `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .`. Reversible, just teaches jj to track `main@origin`.

5. **Re-run push**: `vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."`. Push reads `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` (stage = `push-app`), resumes there, runs `push-app` → `finalize-claude`. Commits + bookmarks from the failed run stand.

**Risks I want to flag:**

- If `jj squash` somehow picks up push-state.toml changes in `@` (e.g. if push wrote to it after `.gitignore` was updated), those would end up in the squashed commit. The bot thinks this won't happen because push hasn't run since the original failure — but let's verify by inspecting `jj st` after the `.gitignore` + `CLAUDE.md` edits, before the squash.

- If the resumed `push-app` still fails (e.g. the `jj bookmark track` didn't fully resolve the issue), we're in the same spot as now — recoverable, just iterate.

- Push's preflight runs again on resume. If the squash produced any state that makes `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test` unhappy, it'll fail at preflight. These are doc-only edits though, so expected clean.

OK to proceed?

Next action on your OK: add /.vc-x1 to .gitignore plus a CLAUDE.md note, squash into the existing commit, track the remote, and resume push. (disable recaps in /config)

I ran into this before and I wonder if something "we" are doing, maybe in `vc-x1 sync` (which I ran before we started) or maybe `vc-x1 push` is messing the the tracking. Thoughts.

content.text:
Two findings from the source:

**Finding 1 — the check exists, just in the wrong place.** `finalize.rs:158–229` has `find_non_tracking_remote` with the exact error message format we just hit (`"bookmark '<b>' has non-tracking remote '<b>@<remote>' — run jj bookmark track ..."`). So finalize pre-flights this. **Push's preflight does not** — `push.rs:784–803` only runs `sync` + `fmt` + `clippy` + `test`. That's inconsistent: push fails with a raw jj error at stage `push-app`, while finalize would refuse earlier with a clean message. The bot thinks moving (or duplicating) the check into push's preflight is a legit vc-x1 improvement worth filing.

**Finding 2 — no code path in sync or push untracks a bookmark.** I scanned both: no `jj bookmark forget`, no `jj bookmark untrack`, no `--no-track` anywhere. `sync` does `jj git fetch` + `jj bookmark set <b> -r <b>@<remote>` (fast-forward) / `jj rebase` — never `untrack` or `forget`. Push calls `jj bookmark set` and `jj git push --bookmark` — also never untracks.

**The bot thinks** this is the common jj behavior, not tool-caused: `jj git fetch` creates remote bookmarks as **non-tracking by default**. They only become tracked once someone runs `jj bookmark track <b>@<remote>` explicitly, and that state is then persisted. If this repo was initialized/cloned in a way that never called `track`, then all fetches since produce a non-tracking `main@origin` that any push will trip on. Sync doesn't untrack — it just never tracks in the first place if tracking wasn't set up.

**Upshot for our current decision:**
- `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .` is safe, correct, and one-time (won't need to re-run on future pushes).
- Worth filing a vc-x1 issue to copy the finalize check into push's preflight — catching this at preflight (before commits happen) is cleaner than catching it at push-app (after commits are locked in locally). This would have saved us from landing in this half-applied state right now.
- Also worth asking: should `vc-x1 init` (or `vc-x1 sync` on first run) auto-track? Probably yes — same reasoning. But out of scope for the current fix.

Still want me to proceed with the plan I outlined (gitignore + CLAUDE.md note → squash → `jj bookmark track` → resume push)?

file.content:
Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a

input.old_string:
## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.**
input.new_string:
## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Prerequisite — one-time per repo:**

- `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's in-progress
  state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get committed.
  Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
- The target bookmark must have a tracking remote
  (`jj bookmark track <bookmark>@origin -R .`). `jj git fetch`
  creates remote bookmarks as non-tracking by default; push
  currently surfaces this as a raw jj error at `push-app` after
  commits are locally made, so confirm tracking is set up
  before the first push in a new clone.

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.**

content.text:
Agreed — matches the "Late changes after push" pattern in CLAUDE.md. Current state: `D .vc-x1/push-state.toml` is pending in `@` (push's finalize-claude stage cleaned it up, producing a deletion we can use).

**Proposed commands** (3 steps, in order):

1. Squash the deletion into the pushed commit (needs `--ignore-immutable` since `10c22009` is already at origin):
   ```
   jj squash --ignore-immutable -R /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/actor-x1
   ```
   This rewrites `10c22009` → new commit without `push-state.toml`. Working copy `@` becomes empty again.

2. Ensure `main` points to the rewritten commit:
   ```
   jj bookmark set main -r @- -R /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/actor-x1
   ```
   (jj usually moves bookmarks automatically on rewrite, but explicit is safe.)

3. Push the rewritten commit — this **force-pushes** (rewrites remote history for `main`):
   ```
   jj git push --bookmark main -R /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/actor-x1
   ```

**Note on risk** — step 3 rewrites the published `main` history. Since this push just happened ~a minute ago and no other collaborator has pulled, the blast radius is zero. But it is a force-push on a shared branch in principle.

`.claude` doesn't need this treatment — `push-state.toml` was only tracked in actor-x1's commit.

OK to run all three?

message.content: LG, before the push we see that squash's indicates with `main*` that it's tracked and changed and ready to be pushed, correct?

content.text:
Yes — `main*` means the local bookmark differs from its tracked remote (`main@origin`). Specifically:
- No `*` → local and remote are in sync
- `*` → local has moved and is ahead/behind/diverged vs the remote

It requires tracking first (otherwise jj wouldn't even compare). So `main*` is exactly the signal "tracked, out of sync with origin, ready for `jj git push` to reconcile." In our case the `jj bookmark list --tracked` output will further show "ahead by 1, behind by 1" (the divergence from the rewrite), which `jj git push` handles as a force-push of the new commit.

attachment.snippet:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	The bot session repo is this project's **`other-repo`** — the
19	path is set by `[workspace].other-repo` in `.vc-config.toml`
20	(default `.claude`, as used here). Concrete examples below use
21	`.claude` for readability; treat the path as configurable where
22	it appears in prose.
23	
24	## Working Directory
25	
26	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
27	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
28	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
29	command failures downstream.
30	
31	## Memory
32	
33	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
34	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
35	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
36	and it loses on discoverability:
37	
38	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
39	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
40	  (human or bot).
41	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
42	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
43	
44	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
45	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
46	
47	## Speculation marker
48	
49	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
50	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
51	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
52	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
53	tell the measured from the inferred.
54	
55	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
56	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
57	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
58	in the written record.
59	
60	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
61	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
62	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
63	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
64	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
65	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
66	
67	## Committing
68	
69	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
70	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
71	visible at the start of the command.
72	
73	### App repo
74	```
75	jj commit -m \
76	"title" \
77	-m "body
78	
79	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
80	-R .
81	```
82	
83	### Bot session repo
84	```
85	jj commit -m \
86	"title" \
87	-m "body
88	
89	ochid: /<changeID>" \
90	-R .claude
91	```
92	
93	## jj Basics
94	
95	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
96	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
97	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
98	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
99	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
100	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
101	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
102	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
103	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
104	  session because session data updates continuously.
105	
106	## Commit Message Style
107	
108	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
109	a version suffix:
110	
111	```
112	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
113	```
114	
115	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
116	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
117	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
118	- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3
119	  sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds
120	  one-to-one with the edits structure already documented in
121	  `notes/chores-*.md` for this step — just the file and a
122	  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not*
123	  restate the detail that lives in chores; the commit body is a
124	  scan-able index, not a duplicate. The chores section is the
125	  source of truth.
126	- **Session notes** (optional tail, appended to the body): if the
127	  session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the
128	  code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends,
129	  rationale — append a trailing subsection titled
130	  `## <other-repo> session notes:` (e.g.
131	  `## .claude session notes:`) with those items as bullets. The
132	  same body (main content plus any session-notes subsection)
133	  lands in both commits.
134	- **Ochid trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you
135	  pass via `--body`. Push appends the correct per-repo trailer
136	  at the commit stages (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>` for the app-repo
137	  commit; `ochid: /<chid>` for the `.claude` commit).
138	- Examples:
139	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
140	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
141	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
142	
143	## Pre-commit Requirements
144	
145	### User approval
146	
147	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
148	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
149	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
150	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
151	
152	### Review before proposing the commit block
153	
154	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
155	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
156	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
157	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
158	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
159	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
160	
161	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
162	review pause before its commit block appears.
163	
164	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
165	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
166	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
167	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
168	step before starting the next; don't ask.
169	
170	### Notes references
171	
172	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
173	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
174	
175	### Versioning
176	
177	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
178	on scope:
179	
180	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
181	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
182	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
183	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
184	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
185	
186	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
187	starting.
188	
189	For multi-step:
190	
191	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
192	   marker.
193	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
194	   needed).
195	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
196	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
197	   marker.
198	
199	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**

... [283 lines truncated] ...

input.new_string:
- **Perf deps move with the perf code.** `hdrhistogram`,
  `minstant`, and `core_affinity` migrate out of actor-x1's
  `Cargo.toml` in `0.2.0-2`; actor-x1 picks them up transitively
  via `tprobe` without redeclaring.

## Workspace layout + tprobe skeleton (0.2.0-1)

Converts the repo from a single crate at the root into a virtual
Cargo workspace with `actor-x1` and a new `tprobe` crate under
`crates/`. No probe code moves yet — `src/perf/*` still lives
inside `actor-x1`, and the binaries / tests are unchanged
behaviorally. This step only reshuffles the file layout so that
`0.2.0-2` can move perf into `tprobe` cleanly.

- `Cargo.toml` (new, root): virtual workspace.
  `[workspace]` with `resolver = "2"`,
  `members = ["crates/actor-x1", "crates/tprobe"]`. No
  `[package]` at the root anymore.
- `crates/actor-x1/Cargo.toml`: former root `Cargo.toml`, moved
  verbatim; version bumped `0.2.0-0` → `0.2.0-1`. Deps
  unchanged (`clap`, `core_affinity`, `hdrhistogram`,
  `minstant`) — they migrate to `tprobe` in `0.2.0-2`.
- `crates/actor-x1/src/`: former root `src/` moved verbatim.
  `lib.rs`, `runtime.rs`, `bin/{goal1,goal2}.rs`, and
  `perf/**` all in place; no content edits.
- `crates/tprobe/Cargo.toml`: new. `name = "tprobe"`,
  `version = "0.1.0-0"` (dev-ladder suffix matching the
  `actor-x1` convention — drops `-N` when perf moves in at
  `0.2.0-2`), same license, empty `[dependencies]`.
- `crates/tprobe/src/lib.rs`: new. `//!` docstring only;
  no public items yet.

### Side effects

- `Cargo.lock` stays at root (workspace-shared); cargo
  regenerated it to recognize the two members. Version lines
  updated automatically.
- `cargo install --path crates/actor-x1` is the new invocation
  for the `goal1` / `goal2` bins (root is virtual so `--path .`
  no longer works for install).
- `target/` stays at the root (workspace-shared); `.gitignore`
  already excludes it, no change needed.
- `notes/`, `README.md`, `LICENSE-*`, `CLAUDE.md`,
  `.vc-config.toml`, `.gitignore` remain at the workspace root.

### Verification

- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean across both
  members.
- `cargo test` — 22 pass (actor-x1) + 0 (tprobe stub) = 22/22;
  same count as `0.2.0-0`, confirming no test was lost in the
  move.
- `cargo install --path crates/actor-x1` replaces the prior
  `actor-x1 v0.2.0-0` install; `goal1 --version` and
  `goal2 --version` both report `actor-x1 0.2.0-1`.

message.content: I was talking with your internet sibiling about how to manage CLAUDE.md going out-of-date as we evolve. They suggested several solutions, the simplest being move CLAUDE.md to ~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md and then have the local CLAUDE.md contain `@~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md`, thoughts

content.text:
The single-source-of-truth goal is right, but the current `CLAUDE.md` explicitly argues against putting durable bot context outside the repo (the Memory section: "hidden under the user's home, tied to one machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed"). Moving the canonical workflow doc to `~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md` reintroduces exactly those drawbacks: a new collaborator cloning the repo gets a broken import; the file isn't reviewable in PRs; version skew between projects becomes untraceable via any one repo's log.

A committed-per-project alternative that still gives you single-edit updates: keep the canonical file in `vc-template-x1` (it's already the source the user copies from), and add a `vc-x1 claude-sync` subcommand (or similar) that copies template → project when invoked. Preserves "committed, reviewable, visible" while eliminating manual duplication. One sibling todo for vc-x1's list, not a runtime import.

Also worth verifying: the file content is `@!/.claude/ws-dual-repo.x.md`, not `@~/.claude/...`. The `!` vs `~` looks like a typo (keyboard-adjacent on some layouts) — and `@!` isn't a Claude Code import syntax I recognize, though `@~` with tilde expansion might be. If Claude Code actually honors `@!`, that's news to me; if not, the local CLAUDE.md currently provides **no** instructions.

The bot thinks: worth checking what the file actually resolves to before relying on it for the next step. Want me to test by starting a fresh session, or fall back to the prior CLAUDE.md content for now?

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

The bot session repo is this project's **`other-repo`** — the
path is set by `[workspace].other-repo` in `.vc-config.toml`
(default `.claude`, as used here). Concrete examples below use
`.claude` for readability; treat the path as configurable where
it appears in prose.

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

---

input.prompt:
In Claude Code, a project-level `CLAUDE.md` can use `@path/to/file.md` syntax to import another file's contents. Question: what happens at session start if the imported target file does not exist?

Specifically:
- Does Claude Code emit a warning (to stderr, to the user-visible UI, or anywhere)?
- Does the session silently proceed with the import unresolved (as if the line weren't there)?
- Does it error out / refuse to start?
- Is there anywhere the user could have seen a diagnostic about the missing import?

Context: a user has `CLAUDE.md` containing only `@~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md`, and wants to know whether a typo in that path would be flagged or silently swallowed. They want to rely on the import to distribute shared instructions across projects; a silent failure would be dangerous because a project could end up with effectively no CLAUDE.md instructions with no indication.

Short answer is fine — under 150 words. Cite the docs if you find the relevant section.

content.text:
Based on the official Claude Code documentation on CLAUDE.md, here's what happens when an imported target file doesn't exist:

**The documentation does not specify the behavior for missing imports.** The relevant section on [importing additional files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-md.md#import-additional-files) states:

> "CLAUDE.md files can import additional files using `@path/to/import` syntax... Imported files are expanded and loaded into context at launch alongside the CLAUDE.md that references them."

It mentions that:
- A dialog appears the **first time** Claude Code encounters external imports, listing the files and asking for approval
- If you decline, "the imports stay disabled and the dialog does not appear again"

However, it does **not** document what happens if the target file is missing—whether Claude silently continues, emits a warning, or errors out.

**Recommendation:** This is a gap in the documentation. Given your use case (relying on imports for distributed instructions), you should test this or raise the question via `/feedback` to get official clarification. A silent failure would indeed be dangerous for your workflow, and it's worth confirming the behavior before depending on it.

**The documentation does not specify the behavior for missing imports.** The relevant section on [importing additional files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-md.md#import-additional-files) states:

> "CLAUDE.md files can import additional files using `@path/to/import` syntax... Imported files are expanded and loaded into context at launch alongside the CLAUDE.md that references them."

It mentions that:
- A dialog appears the **first time** Claude Code encounters external imports, listing the files and asking for approval
- If you decline, "the imports stay disabled and the dialog does not appear again"

However, it does **not** document what happens if the target file is missing—whether Claude silently continues, emits a warning, or errors out.

**Recommendation:** This is a gap in the documentation. The guide agent couldn't find official documentation on missing-import behavior — Claude Code's docs state imports are "expanded and loaded into context at launch" but don't specify what happens when the target is missing (warning, silent drop, error, or something else). The agent recommended testing empirically or using `/feedback` to get clarification.

**Options to derisk without waiting on docs:**

- **Empirical test**: rename `~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md` to `.bak`, start a fresh session, see whether Claude Code complains or loads an effectively-empty CLAUDE.md. One-time check; tells you the actual behavior.
- **Fallback content**: keep a minimal stub in the project-level CLAUDE.md alongside the import line — something like "If you see nothing after this, `~/.claude/ws-dual-repo.md` failed to import; check your setup." If Claude Code silently drops the import, the stub still shows. If it warns, no harm.
- **Switch patterns**: the earlier-discussed canonical-in-`vc-template-x1` + `vc-x1 claude-sync` approach sidesteps this entirely — the content is always physically present in the project.

The bot thinks the fallback-content option is cheapest: one extra line in each project's CLAUDE.md, defense in depth, no new tooling.

content.text:
Expected — `*` tracks bookmark-vs-remote divergence, not working-copy status. Your `main` and `main@origin` both point to `c67a25df` (last pushed commit), so they're in sync → no `*`.

Your unpushed changes live in `@` (the mutable working-copy commit on top of `main`), not on `main` itself. Only a `jj commit` (or explicit `jj bookmark set`) would move `main` forward and produce the `*`. For "do I have pending changes?" use `jj st`; for "is my bookmark out of sync with origin?" use the `*` / `jj bookmark list --tracked`.
